summary:
The Diet of Worms (April 1521 AD) was the imperial assembly at which Martin Luther was summoned to appear before Emperor [[event:2497|Charles V]] and the assembled princes and ecclesiastics of the Holy Roman Empire, given the opportunity to recant his works, and famously refused — delivering one of history's most celebrated stands on individual conscience against institutional authority. Luther, who had already been excommunicated by Pope Leo X in January 1521, was guaranteed safe conduct to appear; his refusal to recant — reportedly concluding with the words 'Here I stand; I can do no other, God help me' (though the precise words are debated by historians) — resulted in the Edict of Worms declaring him an outlaw and heretic of the Empire.

The Diet of Worms's outcome was paradoxically favorable for Luther. Elector Frederick the Wise of Saxony, who had protected Luther throughout, arranged his 'kidnapping' by friendly knights who concealed him in Wartburg Castle for nearly a year — during which Luther translated the New Testament into German, making scripture directly accessible to ordinary German readers. The Edict of Worms was widely ignored by sympathetic German princes, and Charles V's preoccupation with the [[event:2492|Italian Wars]] and the Ottoman threat prevented the energetic enforcement that might have crushed Lutheranism in its infancy. The Diet marked the decisive moment when the [[event:102|Protestant Reformation]] passed from a theological controversy into a political reality: with imperial authority declared but unenforced, the Reformation became a permanent feature of the German religious landscape.
